text: Hi, I'm Dr. Lillian Shapiro. There are many reasons to have a hysterectomy. To some women, keeping your ovaries and or your cervix is a safe option.
The surgeon removes the fallopian tube, carefully excising it to prevent future cancers while preserving the ovaries.
The video shows a normal ovary in the surgical field, with the fallopian tube visible nearby.
The camera shows a round, white ovary in the background, with visible follicles indicating the patient is not in menopause.
The surgeon disconnects the uterus at the level of the round ligament, followed by the disconnection of the bladder from the uterus.
The video shows the bladder positioned directly on top of the uterus, with a visible fibroid causing pressure on the bladder, which is likely contributing to the patient's symptoms.
The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder away from the uterus, revealing the fibroid that is causing the patient's symptoms.
The surgeon separates the cervix, which will remain with the patient, from the fibroid uterus, using precise dissection techniques.
I have had patients not take any narcotics following their robotic hysterectomy. Of course, everybody reacts differently.

Patients decide with their nurse if they would like to stay overnight or to go home immediately from the recovery room. This is not an option for everybody and has to be discussed on an individual basis.
The uterine specimen is completely detached from the cervix, visible in the surgical field.
If you are thinking about having a hysterectomy and looking for more opinions, please give us a call and I would be happy to sit down with you and discuss all of your options.
